task: I’ve got to roll out a small process change to my team and I want the message to be clear without turning into a big speech. Can you help me draft something I could send in a quick note, plus a shorter version I could use if I need to say it out loud?

prefs.explanation: He is driven by results and status, so he likely prefers help that gets to the point quickly. A softened or overly diplomatic style would probably feel inefficient to him. He would respond better to direct wording that makes the action or issue immediately clear.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: His work style is efficiency-focused, and he likely values responses that save time rather than unpack everything. Long-winded help would feel like drag unless the extra detail clearly affects the outcome. He probably wants the shortest answer that still lets him act decisively.

prefs.short: Likes responses in clean bullet points or checklists
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that can be scanned and used fast, especially in a managerial setting. A structured format fits his control-oriented style and reduces ambiguity. He would likely dislike rambling prose when a simple checklist would do.

prefs.short: Can tolerate firm pushback if it protects results
prefs.explanation: He is not looking for emotional cushioning, so a direct warning about risk would likely be acceptable if it is practical. Because he has already been burned by compliance problems, he may respect pushback that prevents mistakes. He would still resist pushback that feels like moralizing or slows execution without clear benefit.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over fancy wording
prefs.explanation: He seems likely to value clear, practical language that reads like business communication rather than polished rhetoric. Jargon is fine only if it is useful and common in the setting. Overly formal or literary phrasing would probably feel unnecessary to him.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a clean, direct version you can send as a quick note, plus a shorter spoken version.

**Quick note:**
“Team, we’re making a small process change starting [date]. Going forward, [brief description of the change]. This is to help us [reason/goal]. Please make sure you follow the new process and let me know if you have any questions.”

**Short version to say out loud:**
“Quick update: starting [date], we’re changing [process] to help us [goal]. Please follow the new steps, and come to me if anything’s unclear.”

If you want, I can also make it sound more **formal, firm, or friendly** depending on your team.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "here’s a clean, direct version" and the drafts get straight to the action without fluff.
Concise throughout; it gives only the note and spoken version, with no long explanation.
Uses clean structure with labeled sections: "Quick note:" and "Short version to say out loud:" plus a simple offer to adjust tone.
Includes mild pushback/authority with "Please make sure you follow the new process" and "come to me if anything’s unclear," but it stays cooperative rather than strongly challenging.
Plain English throughout: "small process change," "starting [date]," "follow the new process"—clear business language without fancy wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter version that sounds like you’re leading the room.

**1) Quick note**
Team, starting [date], we’re changing the process for [process]. Going forward, [new process]. This is now the standard, so make sure you’re following it.

**2) Spoken version**
Quick update: starting [date], we’re changing how we handle [process]. The new steps are [brief summary]. This is the process going forward, so make sure it’s followed.

If you want, I can make it even more like a branch manager huddle — sharper and more natural.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "here’s a tighter version" and the drafts get straight to "starting [date], we’re changing..." with no fluff.
Concise throughout. It gives a quick note and a spoken version in just a few sentences, matching the preference for brevity.
Uses a clean structured format with "1) Quick note" and "2) Spoken version," making it easy to scan. Minor gap: it’s not a checklist, but still well organized.
Includes firm, practical pushback: "This is now the standard" and "make sure it’s followed." It’s direct without moralizing.
Plain English throughout: "changing the process," "new steps," "make sure it’s followed." No fancy wording or jargon.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s that same tone, tightened up and ready to use.

**1) Quick note**  
Team, starting Monday, we’re changing how we handle loan file review. Every file gets checked before it moves forward — no exceptions. This keeps us clean on compliance and cuts down on rework. Make sure it’s done right the first time.

**2) Spoken version**  
Quick update: starting Monday, loan files get reviewed before they move. No skipping steps. We need clean files and no surprises later.

If you want, I can give you 3 more versions in the same style — one a little firmer, one more neutral, and one for a specific process change.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "starting Monday," "Every file gets checked before it moves forward — no exceptions," "No skipping steps."
Concise throughout; gives a short note and an even shorter spoken version without extra explanation.
Uses clean structure with labeled sections: "1) Quick note" and "2) Spoken version," making it easy to scan.
Includes firm pushback that protects results, e.g. "no exceptions" and "No skipping steps," but it stays practical rather than moralizing.
Plain English, businesslike wording: "clean on compliance," "cuts down on rework," "no surprises later." No fancy phrasing.
